phrasalVerbs.definition: To remove something from a larger piece by cutting.
examples: She cut out pictures from the magazine for her project.
I need to cut out a hole in the wall for the pipe.
He cut out a piece of cardboard for the model.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To destroy a building or wall.
examples: They knocked down the old warehouse to build flats.
We're planning to tear down the garden shed.
The council pulled down the abandoned factory.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To fix something such as equipment in the place where it will be used and make it ready to use.
examples: We're having a new kitchen put in next week.
The plumber put in a new boiler yesterday.
They put in double-glazed windows last month.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To make something by joining all its parts.
examples: It took me all afternoon to put together the bookshelf.
Can you help me put together this desk?
She put together a beautiful photo album.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To build something such as a wall, fence, or house.
examples: They put up a new fence around the garden.
The builders are putting up a new block of flats.
We put up some shelves in the living room.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To build a structure or put it in a particular place.
examples: They set up a tent in the garden for the party.
We need to set up the stage before the concert.
He set up the scaffolding around the house.

fillInExercises.sentence: She ___ ___ shapes from coloured paper for the decoration.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Cut out' means to remove by cutting.

fillInExercises.sentence: They're going to ___ down the old building next month.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Knock down' means to demolish a structure.

fillInExercises.sentence: We're having a new bathroom ___ ___ next week.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Put in' means to install equipment or fixtures.

fillInExercises.sentence: It took hours to ___ together the flat-pack furniture.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Put together' means to assemble something from its parts.

fillInExercises.sentence: The neighbours ___ ___ a new fence last weekend.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Put up' means to build a structure like a fence or wall.

fillInExercises.sentence: We need to ___ up the marquee before the guests arrive.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Set up' means to build or arrange a structure in place.

fillInExercises.sentence: They ___ ___ the old factory to make way for new houses.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Tear down' means to destroy or demolish a building.

fillInExercises.sentence: Can you help me ___ out this section of the carpet?
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Cut out' means to remove a piece by cutting.

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: We're finally going to [put in|put in] a new kitchen!

lines.speaker: Sarah
lines.text: That's exciting! Are you going to [knock/pull/tear down|knock/pull/tear down] any walls?

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: Yes, we'll [knock/pull/tear down|knock/pull/tear down] the wall between the kitchen and dining room.

lines.speaker: Sarah
lines.text: Nice! Will you [put up|put up] new cabinets yourself?

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: I'll try. Last time I [put together|put together] a bookshelf, it took ages.

lines.speaker: Sarah
lines.text: Ha! Just make sure to [cut out|cut out] the measurements correctly.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.text: I want to [put up|put up] a new garden shed this weekend.

lines.speaker: Fatima
lines.text: That sounds like a big job. First, you'll need to [set up|set up] the base.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.text: Right. And I'll have to [knock/pull/tear down|knock/pull/tear down] the old one first.

lines.speaker: Fatima
lines.text: Do you need to [cut out|cut out] any panels for windows?

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.text: Yes, I'll [cut out|cut out] spaces for two windows.

lines.speaker: Fatima
lines.text: Once you [put together|put together] the frame, I can help with the roof.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.text: Perfect! We might even [put in|put in] electricity later.
